segments.text: Hello and welcome. We're excited for you
to join us today. I'm going to show
something really exciting. How you can
use generative AI to extract more value
from your data with BigQuery. I'm Jeff
Nelson, a developer relations engineer
at Google Cloud.
If you aren't already familiar, BigQuery
is our data analytics platform. And
what's cool is that it's integrated with
other Google Cloud services like our
unified AI development platform, Vert.Ex
AI. This allows you to access powerful
foundation models like the Gemini family
right from your BigQuery environment.
And the best part for a lot of folks is
you can do all of this using familiar
SQL syntax. So imagine you have
structured bigquery tables or even
unstructured multimodal data like PDFs,
images, audio and video. You can feed
that data along with a prompt and the
Gemini model returns a response to
BigQuery.
To access these models in BigQuery, you
can call an ML or AI function and supply
it with a few input arguments, namely
the model, a prompt, and some data.
Gemini models take the prompt and data
and generate a response for each row,
returning the results to BigQuery in a
structured and easy to consume format.
You can then use these responses
alongside your existing BigQuery tables
or for ad hoc
analysis. The examples I just mentioned
used Gemini models, but we also provide
model choice. So in addition to Gemini
and Google embedding models, you can
also call partner models like Claude,
Llama, and Mistro models as a service.
And lastly, you can access open models
hosted on Vert.exai. X AI endpoints from
BigQuery. These might include ones you
pull from hugging face or even fine-tune
models specific to your
business. We're also very excited to
announce structured data output in
BigQuery. In many scenarios, you'd want
to return specific fields and data types
back from your large language model so
it matches a certain schema.
You can now define the output schema in
the query and return the columns you ask
for. BigQuery also works with multimodal
data. So in addition to your structured
fields in your tables, you can even
reference unstructured data living in
Google Cloud Storage like images, audio,
documents, and more. And you can mix
that structured and unstructured data
together in a single inference query.
Lastly, you have the flexibility to
apply generative AI to all rows within a
table, or you can choose to only run
inference on a small subset of
records. Now that we've covered some of
the key BigQuery generative AI features,
let's move to today's demo scenario to
see everything in
action. Imagine that you work for a
retailer called Symbol Pets. Your
company sells pet related products like
dog toys or bird seed or cat food, but
your website lacks valuable metadata
like basic product information or search
keywords which makes it hard for your
customers to search and navigate and you
have some unstructured data like product
images or customer reviews but you're
unsure how to extract value from them.
Let's see how we can use BigQuery and
Gemini models to populate the rest of
our
website. In this demo, we'll generate
product
metadata, frequently asked questions
from product
manuals, brand descriptions from images
and text, and lastly, we'll show some
new search techniques to enhance the
website. All right, so let's orient
ourselves and begin in the Google Cloud
Console. And next I'll move over to
BigQuery. In the BigQuery Explorer pane,
I have a data set called symbol pets and
it contains a number of tables related
to my business. Tables like customers,
employees, orders, products, and more. I
can check out one of these tables, the
products table for example, and see
fields like product name, brand,
description. But I notice that we don't
have a subcategory.
We don't know what animal type the
product is for and we don't have any
search keywords. So, it's hard for
people to find these products from
search engines. Let's work on populating
this data. Now, I begin by creating
something called an object table.
Actually, I'll create a few of them. And
an object table is simply a way for
BigQuery to access unstructured data in
cloud storage. So I passed the URI which
is a path to where the objects reside in
cloud
storage and this location contains our
product image catalog. I could head over
to this bucket and we could check those
images
out. For example, I can click on one of
these images and here I see what looks
to be an aquarium.
So again, this is a bucket full of my
product images and these object tables
provide a SQL interface to those images
in cloud storage. We're not moving data
out of cloud storage. We're just
referencing it. I also have a separate
object table, one for product manuals,
and we'll check that one out in a
minute. Now, when I query an object
table, I return back some information.
We see fields like the URI that they're
images some object metadata and
importantly this ref column which
includes elements like a URI an
authorizer and some details. This ref
column is known as an object ref and
it's automatically added to new object
tables. The ref column can also reside
in standard BigQuery tables. An object
ref is a new data type that forms the
foundation for how we access
unstructured objects for AI purposes. So
keep it in mind as we move
on. I also create a pointer to the
Gemini 2.0
flash. And this endpoint is hosted in
Vertex AI. So we're not creating a new
model. We're not copying a model. We're
simply pointing to an existing model so
we can access it from BigQuery.
Next, I'll begin to query my
unstructured data with a pretty simple
example. The AI.generate table function
takes a few inputs. The Gemini model we
just defined, the object table which
points to our product images, and a
prompt asking to describe each product
image in a single sentence. I also
specify the a number of optional
arguments like temperature and max
output tokens. You might be familiar
with some of these ladder arguments if
you've used large language models, but
rest assured that these are optional and
BigQuery has default values for each
one. And there we have it. Single
sentence descriptions for each of our
product
images. For example, the first record
describes a glass aquarium on a dark
brown wooden base. And we can confirm
the output is accurate over in Google
Cloud Storage by checking out the image.
We'll take a look. And that looks pretty
accurate. So this is great. AI generate
table will help us generate text based
on our structured or unstructured
inputs. Moving to an example that's a
little bit more complex. For each image,
I could also generate the animal type,
like is it for a dog or a cat, a list of
five likely search keywords, and a
product
subcategory. I can also define the
structure of my outputs. I'll ask for
three fields and define their data
types. This is great because it helps
eliminate the uncertainty of model
responses and ensures that I return data
that fits my table.
I'll run this query and the response
will help us populate the products table
and our downstream
website. As we saw before, these values
were blank for fields like animal type
or
subcategory. Taking the aquarium
example, we can see the assigned animal
type is a fish, a list of five logical
search keywords, and a subcategory as
aquariums.
Now, it would take countless hours to
pour through hundreds or thousands of
images and generate all of this by hand.
So, we're saving a lot of time here by
using the Gemini models. And of course,
we can then update our products table
with this
info. We can also start to seed
frequently asked questions on our
website given unstructured assets, maybe
using product manuals. A few minutes
ago, we created an object table over a
bucket containing product manuals. So,
let's take a quick look at one of them.
Here we see a product manual for an
automatic pet feeder. It contains a
bunch of information on how to use it
and other
technicalities. So, let's ask the Gemini
model for three common question and
answers that customers might have about
the product.
We'll run the query and the results are
question answer pairs that are derived
from the manual. This was done over just
one manual for this demo, but the
process is exactly the same if you have
thousands of PDFs and will scale as
expected. This is the type of info we
can use to populate our website where
there might otherwise be blank space.
And this example is taken straight from
a product manual, but we could also
incorporate textual customer reviews or
other modalities in the same query to
further enhance the
output. So far, we've mostly taken our
unstructured references from object
tables. But we can also generate these
reference columns called object refs on
standard BigQuery tables.
In this example, I'll generate an object
ref column on my products table. Adding
object ref columns on standard BigQuery
tables allows us to run multimodal
analysis over any table. Here I passed
the URI field alongside a connection
through a make ref function and then
pass that through the fetch metadata
function to get some Google Cloud
Storage
metadata. And then I call this new
column image ref because it points to my
images. The result is my products table
with an object ref column called image
ref. From here I can do some really
unique things. For example, imagine that
we want each brand to have a short
statement on the symbol pets website.
This query passes all of the images and
all of the text descriptions from my new
multimodal table into the Gemini model.
The model then returns a succinct
description for each brand. For example,
you can see that the Aqua Clear brand
has a description that was generated
from an array of 33 images and 33
product descriptions.
We could scale this out to thousands of
imi images and text in a single call.
Another interesting application is using
AI operations to filter tables. Here's
an example. I know my dog enjoys a
certain brand of treats. I know it's
highly rated and it costs less than
$10. I can filter the table using
standard wear clauses, but we still end
up with 20 results.
So I can add an AI.generate bool scalar
function filter as well so that only
rows where the conditions are true are
returned. In this case, I know that my
dog's treats come in a blue bag. So I'm
going to prompt for the image has a blue
bag of food. I'll run the query and the
results come back. Instead of 20 items,
now I'm down to one.
This method of searching using
traditional wear clauses combined with
AI powered row operations is super
powerful because sometimes default
e-commerce filters just don't meet all
of the customer
needs. And we're not just limited to
using the Gemini family of models within
BigQuery. In this example, I'll create a
pointer to a Quen QWQ 32 billion
parameter model hosted on a Vertex AI
endpoint. I'm going to kick off this
query and we'll return to it in just a
second. I'll head over to Vert.ex AI in
the console. And within Vertex AI, we're
able to choose from hundreds of popular
open models, including over a 100,000 we
can deploy straight from
HuggingFace. In fact, I chose this Quen
32B model because it was trending on
HuggingFace. You can see I've already
deployed the Quen 32B model in Vert.Ex
AI and it's running on an endpoint on a
GPU.
This query extracts two to three key
product themes across thousands of
customer reviews. And this time I'm
using the Quen model, not a Gemini
model. And we can take a look at the
results. So for any one of these
products, we see some of our key themes
that our C customers
emphasized. This is a nice summary so a
new user can get a sense of products
without needing to read through
thousands of reviews. And this works not
only for open models but also for
fine-tuned models that might be better
for your business use
case. Now I also want to talk about
search. The BigQuery search function
lets you find unique data elements that
are buried in structured and
semistructured
data. If I want to search for something
like a dog toy, I could use the BigQuery
search function to look for the keyword
in the description field. And when I do
that, I return seven results. This is
because the description field contains
the words dog and toy seven times. But
if I change my query to puppy toy, I
don't return any results because no
description field contains the string
puppy and toy. This is a problem with
keyword search. We only return results
based on exact keywords. And that's
where we can look toward semantic
search. Semantic search uses vector
embeddings to search rather than
keywords. And these embeddings capture
the meaning and the intent behind the
query instead of just literal
keywords. So I'll create a new big query
model which again just points to a text
embedding model hosted in Vert.Ex AI.
And I'll show you what an embedding
looks like. An embedding for puppy toy
turns out to be an array of numbers that
doesn't really mean much to humans, but
actually captures a ton of semantic
information. We'll use this to search
instead of
keywords. These embeddings allow us to
run a vector search. And
specifically, I have this embedding
subquery that gives me the same value
that we just looked at. But now I'm
passing it through the vector search
function against the products table to
which I've added an embedding column.
And I specify to return only the top
three
results. So when I run the vector
search, I actually get results. Now,
although no description has the word
puppy in it, our vector embeddings infer
the intent behind my query and return
appropriate products, the results are
ordered by distance, calculated from
vector embeddings, and a higher score is
a better result. I see some toys that a
puppy would love, and these are all
items that didn't show up earlier with
the standard search function. So, our
search results are improving and this
might be something to implement on our
site. Now, I'd like to check the product
images for my results because simply
reading the descriptions isn't great for
visualizing. So, I'll copy this query
and move over to a BigQuery Collab
notebook where you'll see there's
already a little bit of Python set up. I
just wrote a simple function to render
the images returned from a vector
search. So I can paste my query into a
cell within this notebook and specify to
return the result as a data frame called
text results. When I print text results,
I get the same information back as what
we previously saw in the SQL editor. But
I can also pass this new data frame into
my display product images function and
check out the results. This is pretty
awesome because I can now use Python and
SQL together to visualize my search
right within
BigQuery. Now this previous example
compared embeddings from text to text,
but we can also make this multimodal.
I'll define another remote model in
BigQuery that references the multimodal
embedding model
endpoint and almost identical to the
last query. I'll turn my search query
into a multimodal embedding as a
subquery, but then I'll pass it through
the vector search function to my table
with images and embeddings. So now I'm
searching text to image and I'll display
the top results for fish tank. So, I'll
search for the top three results and we
can check them out. And when we check
out the results, we are able to
visualize them. One really neat
application here is that I don't need to
input the string fish tank necessarily.
I could instead replace it with an image
or audio or a video and still return
results. This enables multimodal search
like image to text or video to image and
all other combinations. and it opens up
a whole new world of possibilities for
users to search our
site. With that, we'll end the demo and
move back over to the
slides. I'd just like to recap what we
did and provide you with some resources
to try yourself. We used a number of
different unstructured data sources like
images, PDFs, and text customer reviews.
And we generated valuable data that
helps our business and
discoverability, namely additional
product information from images. We
seated some basic frequently asked
questions. We generated brand
descriptions from images and text. And
lastly, we looked at alternative ways to
search using embeddings. We did all of
this in BigQuery and with the help of
Gemini and open
models. While we displayed a number of
generative AI capabilities today, there
are plenty more, and I'd encourage you
to think about what you might apply to
your data. Here you'll find some
information to get started. First, some
documentation and examples about using
new BigQuery generative AI functions
over your data. Next, additional
information about deploying and using
open models with BigQuery. And last, how
to get started with object ref to
analyze structured and unstructured data
together in the same tables. And
finally, on behalf of myself and
everyone on the Big Query and Vertex AI
teams who made all of this incredible
functionality possible, we'd like to
extend a big thank you for your time. I
hope you're as excited as we are about
getting hands-on and using generative
models for your own use cases. We look
forward to hearing from you about the
problems you solve with these features
and what you build with them. Enjoy the
rest of your time at Google IO and thank
you for joining us.
[Music]
